perturbation.original_text: 5.8 At all times while this Agreement is in effect, Company shall carry insurance of the types and in the minimum amounts set forth in Exhibit "G". All such insurance set forth in Exhibit "G" shall specifically name BP as an additional insured or provide that the insurer shall waive all rights of subrogation against BP.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.8 At all times while this Agreement is in effect, Company shall carry insurance. All such insurance shall specifically name BP as an additional insured.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for 'the types and in the minimum amounts set forth in Exhibit G' and 'provide that the insurer shall waive all rights of subrogation against BP', the contract becomes non-compliant with standard insurance requirements for oil and gas operations in Louisiana, thus increasing BP's risk exposure. Louisiana law requires specific coverage amounts depending on the nature of the operation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Louisiana Revised Statutes 30:28 pertains to the requirement of insurance coverage for oil and gas wells. By not stating the types or minimum amounts of insurance required, the contract violates this statute. It creates ambiguity and reduces protection for potential liabilities, such as pollution or damages, and fails to ensure comprehensive coverage as required by state regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the specific insurance requirements detailed in Exhibit G and waives subrogation rights against BP. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this omission makes the contract non-compliant with standard insurance practices for oil and gas operations in Louisiana, increasing BP's risk exposure. The Law Explanation states that by not specifying the types or minimum amounts of insurance, the contract violates Louisiana Revised Statutes 30:28, leading to ambiguity and reduced protection for potential liabilities. Snippet 2 mentions 'Contract Lifecycle Management Platform of the Year' in the context of LexisNexis, suggesting the importance of comprehensive contract management, which this modified contract fails to achieve due to the omission of crucial insurance details.

perturbation.original_text: 5.6 Unless Company elects by thirty (30) days' prior written notice to BP either to take in kind or to separately dispose of its share of oil, gas and other hydrocarbons, BP shall in good faith, to the extent it can do so, cause Company's share of production from the Initial Well and each Substitute Well and Additional Wells to be marketed and sold to either a Third Party or to an Affiliate of BP in a commercially reasonable manner, which terms shall not be less than on the same terms and conditions as BP's share of production from such wells are sold. It is recognized by the Parties that BP, or its predecessor, has provided at its cost or made arrangements with Third Parties to provide certain facilities beyond the wellhead (the "Plants") needed for producing, storing, separating, gathering, treating, processing and delivering production from the Initial Well and each Substitute Well and Additional Well. It is agreed that BP will continue to make the Plants (as they or any contractual arrangements related thereto may be modified, changed or upgraded) proportionately available to handle BP, Company and Third Party production from the Contract Area. It is understood that a proportionate share of the cost of maintaining and operating the Plants, including depreciation or rental in lieu of depreciation and actual Third Party costs, whether on a cash fee basis or on a retained volume basis, will be allocated to the Parties on a "throughput" basis (being that portion of such costs relating to the production volumes from the Initial Well or, if drilled, any Substitute Well or Additional Well, as each may bear to the total production volumes handled by the Plants, including any Third Party or BP volumes not produced from the Initial Well or, if drilled, any Substitute Well or Additional Well). Nothing herein shall be construed to impart, transfer or convey any ownership interest in the Plants to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.6 Unless Company elects to take in kind or to separately dispose of its share of oil, gas and other hydrocarbons, BP shall cause Company's share of production from the Initial Well and each Substitute Well and Additional Wells to be marketed and sold to either a Third Party or to an Affiliate of BP. It is recognized by the Parties that BP, or its predecessor, has provided at its cost or made arrangements with Third Parties to provide certain facilities beyond the wellhead (the "Plants") needed for producing, storing, separating, gathering, treating, processing and delivering production from the Initial Well and each Substitute Well and Additional Well. It is agreed that BP will continue to make the Plants (as they or any contractual arrangements related thereto may be modified, changed or upgraded) proportionately available to handle BP, Company and Third Party production from the Contract Area. It is understood that a proportionate share of the cost of maintaining and operating the Plants, including depreciation or rental in lieu of depreciation and actual Third Party costs, whether on a cash fee basis or on a retained volume basis, will be allocated to the Parties on a "throughput" basis (being that portion of such costs relating to the production volumes from the Initial Well or, if drilled, any Substitute Well or Additional Well, as each may bear to the total production volumes handled by the Plants, including any Third Party or BP volumes not produced from the Initial Well or, if drilled, any Substitute Well or Additional Well). Nothing herein shall be construed to impart, transfer or convey any ownership interest in the Plants to Company.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the clause "in good faith, to the extent it can do so, cause Company's share of production from the Initial Well and each Substitute Well and Additional Wells to be marketed and sold to either a Third Party or to an Affiliate of BP in a commercially reasonable manner, which terms shall not be less than on the same terms and conditions as BP's share of production from such wells are sold," the contract becomes ambiguous on the obligation for BP to deal fairly with True North in selling the production from the wells. This violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing that Louisiana law imposes on all contracts. This duty implies that each party will act in a manner that does not deprive the other party of the benefits of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Louisiana Civil Code Article 1983 states that "Contracts must be performed in good faith." The omission of the requirement for BP to sell True North's share of production on commercially reasonable terms and under the same conditions as its own production violates this implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. It allows BP to potentially prioritize its own interests, potentially harming True North financially.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated BP to act 'in good faith' and in a 'commercially reasonable manner' when marketing True North's share of production. The changed text removes this obligation, creating a potential contradiction with the implied duty of good faith as described in the 'Law Explanation' and supported by the 'Wex' snippet which defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards.' By removing the 'good faith' and 'commercially reasonable manner' requirements, BP could prioritize its own interests, potentially harming True North financially. This omission weakens True North's protections under the agreement, leading to a direct contradiction under Louisiana law which states that 'Contracts must be performed in good faith,' as per the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 13.4 To the extent applicable to the Properties, Company hereby waives the provisions of the Louisiana Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law (La. R.S. 51:1402, et seq.). Company warrants and represents that it: (a) is experienced and knowledgeable with respect to the oil and gas industry generally and with transactions of this type specifically; (b) posses ample knowledge, experience and expertise to evaluate independently the merits and risks of the transactions herein contemplated; and (c) is not in a significantly disparate bargaining position.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.4 To the extent applicable to the Properties, Company hereby waives the provisions of the Louisiana Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law (La. R.S. 51:1402, et seq.). Company warrants and represents that it: (a) is experienced and knowledgeable with respect to the oil and gas industry generally and with transactions of this type specifically; (b) possesses ample knowledge, experience and expertise to evaluate independently the merits and risks of the transactions herein contemplated; and (c) is not in a significantly disparate bargaining position. Company waives all rights to seek recourse for environmental damages caused by BP's operations, regardless of BP's negligence or fault.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a clause stating "Company waives all rights to seek recourse for environmental damages caused by BP's operations, regardless of BP's negligence or fault," the contract attempts to waive rights to legal recourse that are considered non-waivable under Louisiana environmental laws and regulations. This directly contradicts Louisiana law that holds operators liable for environmental damages and requires them to restore affected areas.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Louisiana Environmental Quality Act, La. R.S. 30:2001 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: Louisiana's Environmental Quality Act (La. R.S. 30:2001 et seq.) establishes liability for environmental damages and provides for restoration requirements. Attempting to waive rights to recourse for environmental damage, even in the absence of negligence or fault, is a violation of this act as it undermines the legal framework designed to protect the environment and hold responsible parties accountable.

perturbation.justification: The added clause in the changed text, "Company waives all rights to seek recourse for environmental damages caused by BP's operations, regardless of BP's negligence or fault," directly contradicts the legal obligation to hold responsible parties accountable for environmental damage, as explained in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. Although there are no valid scraped law snippets, the Law Explanation states that Louisiana's Environmental Quality Act (La. R.S. 30:2001 et seq.) establishes liability for environmental damages and provides for restoration requirements. The change attempts to waive these rights, which undermines the legal framework. This establishes a clear contradiction under Omission Legal Contradiction.
